channel.title: TechNode
channel.description: Latest news and trends about tech in China

item.title: Capcom-licensed Monster Hunter Outlanders mobile game starts test in China on April 29
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: The mobile game Monster Hunter Outlanders, licensed by Capcom and developed by Tencent’s TiMi Studio Group, will begin its testing phase on April 29. This test will be a limited-availability, account-restricted, data-wipe beta with in-game purchases enabled, and is only open to Android devices equipped with Snapdragon 845 or higher processors and running Android 12.0 […]
item.content:encoded:
The mobile game Monster Hunter Outlanders, licensed by Capcom and developed by Tencent’s TiMi Studio Group, will begin its testing phase on April 29. This test will be a limited-availability, account-restricted, data-wipe beta with in-game purchases enabled, and is only open to Android devices equipped with Snapdragon 845 or higher processors and running Android 12.0 or above. As the first licensed Monster Hunter action mobile game in mainland China, the game recreates classic elements of the series, including iconic weapons such as the great sword and long sword, as well as core mechanics such as part-based damage and weak-point targeting.

It also introduces original features such as a companion hunting system and an open-world ecosystem set on Esuo Island. Currently, players can register for participation through the official website. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: Dreame CEO criticizes Xiaohongshu for toxic model and distorted content ecosystem
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Last night, Yu Hao, CEO of Dreame Technology, posted a series of strong statements on his personal Weibo account, openly criticizing the Xiaohongshu platform in sharp and forceful terms. He said the platform suffers from serious issues in overall quality and its social value orientation, even calling it “a very bad platform,” which quickly drew […]
item.content:encoded:
Last night, Yu Hao, CEO of Dreame Technology, posted a series of strong statements on his personal Weibo account, openly criticizing the Xiaohongshu platform in sharp and forceful terms. He said the platform suffers from serious issues in overall quality and its social value orientation, even calling it “a very bad platform,” which quickly drew public attention. Yu further argued that Xiaohongshu’s value system and monetization model are toxic, claiming that its traffic growth is not driven by high-quality content or authentic user experiences, but rather by exploiting darker aspects of human behavior to sustain engagement.

The platform has long tolerated luxury flaunting, status comparison, exaggerated marketing, and fake product recommendations and has created a distorted content ecosystem that deviates from positive social values, he added. [TechWeb, in Chinese]

item.title: Intel warns China of severe server CPU shortage as AI demand surges
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Intel has recently issued a warning to major cloud service providers in China that, due to a surge in demand for AI infrastructure and slower-than-expected yield ramp-up for its 18A process, server CPU supply will face severe shortages over the next two quarters (Q2 and Q3). Delivery lead times for some models could extend up […]
item.content:encoded:
Intel has recently issued a warning to major cloud service providers in China that, due to a surge in demand for AI infrastructure and slower-than-expected yield ramp-up for its 18A process, server CPU supply will face severe shortages over the next two quarters (Q2 and Q3). Delivery lead times for some models could extend up to six months.

Although Intel expects supply to gradually stabilize by the end of the year, the shortage may persist longer. Some companies have already shifted to competing products from AMD or adjusted the pace of their data center expansion. Industry analysts predict that capacity bottlenecks could last until early 2027. [IThome, in Chinese]

item.title: DeepSeek V4 becomes default model for OpenClaw
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: The global open source agent framework OpenClaw released its 2026.4.24 version on Sunday, bringing both DeepSeek V4 models into its core ecosystem. V4 Flash, designed with 284 billion parameters and 13 billion activated parameters, is now the default model, delivering Max mode inference performance close to the 1.6 trillion parameter V4 Pro. Meanwhile, V4 Pro, […]
item.content:encoded: The global open source agent framework OpenClaw released its 2026.4.24 version on Sunday, bringing both DeepSeek V4 models into its core ecosystem. V4 Flash, designed with 284 billion parameters and 13 billion activated parameters, is now the default model, delivering Max mode inference performance close to the 1.6 trillion parameter V4 Pro. Meanwhile, V4 Pro, recognized as the world’s largest open source model, has also been added to the model library. The update introduces real time voice integration with Google Meet, enhances browser automation recovery, and resolves issues in multi step tool execution. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: Li Auto signs UAE, Saudi partners and expands in Asia Pacific
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Li Auto said it has signed partnership agreements with the UAE’s Al Fahim Motors and Saudi Arabia’s Mohamed Yousuf Naghi Motors, marking its planned entry into the Middle East with its extended-range electric L-series models. The company said the rollout will bring its family-focused vehicles to the UAE and Saudi markets. Li Auto also announced […]
item.content:encoded:
Li Auto said it has signed partnership agreements with the UAE’s Al Fahim Motors and Saudi Arabia’s Mohamed Yousuf Naghi Motors, marking its planned entry into the Middle East with its extended-range electric L-series models. The company said the rollout will bring its family-focused vehicles to the UAE and Saudi markets.

Li Auto also announced market entry plans in Cambodia, Laos, Macau, and Myanmar through local distribution partners, with preparations underway for launches starting in May. The move signals a broader acceleration of the company’s overseas expansion across the Middle East and Asia Pacific.

Li Auto added that it plans to enter more markets, including Europe and broader Southeast Asia, and has confirmed it will attend the 2026 Paris Motor Show. [TechNode reporting]

item.title: DeepSeek-V4 preview now available with open-source access
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: DeepSeek released a preview version of its new V4 model series and made it open source. The model supports an ultra-long context of up to one million tokens and shows improvements in agent capabilities, knowledge coverage, and reasoning performance. The V4 series comes in two versions: Pro and Flash. The Pro version targets high-performance use […]
item.content:encoded:
DeepSeek released a preview version of its new V4 model series and made it open source. The model supports an ultra-long context of up to one million tokens and shows improvements in agent capabilities, knowledge coverage, and reasoning performance. The V4 series comes in two versions: Pro and Flash. The Pro version targets high-performance use cases, with overall capabilities approaching those of some leading proprietary models. The Flash version focuses more on efficiency and cost control, making it suitable for scenarios with tighter compute and budget constraints.

The models are currently available via DeepSeek’s official website and app. The API has also been updated to support compatibility with OpenAI and Anthropic interface standards. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: Auto China 2026: Lynk & Co GT concept sports car makes global debut
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: On the opening day of the Beijing International Auto Show, Lynk & Co’s first GT concept sports car made its global debut. The new vehicle features a two-door, wide-body GT layout with low-slung proportions and a flowing silhouette. Its surfaces mix smooth transitions with sharper sculpted details, while the pronounced wheel arches and fastback rear […]
item.content:encoded:
On the opening day of the Beijing International Auto Show, Lynk & Co’s first GT concept sports car made its global debut. The new vehicle features a two-door, wide-body GT layout with low-slung proportions and a flowing silhouette. Its surfaces mix smooth transitions with sharper sculpted details, while the pronounced wheel arches and fastback rear give it a more aggressive stance. At the back, it uses a full-width taillight design paired with a triangular rear window, continuing Lynk & Co’s urban opposition aesthetics design direction.

Lynk & Co is positioning the model as an entry into the high-performance GT space. The brand has been building its motorsport presence through programs such as the LPCC Performance Car Club and its 03+ TCR racing activities. However, currently, no details have been released regarding the car’s powertrain specifications or production timeline. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: TSMC Exec says ASML €350 million lithography tool too expensive, no purchase planned
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: On Thursday, TSMC’s Co-Chief Operating Officer Zhang Xiaoqiang stated at the North America Technology Symposium that the company has no plans to purchase ASML’s latest High-NA EUV lithography machines, citing their price of over €350 million per unit as too high. Although the equipment can improve chip manufacturing precision to below 1.4nm, TSMC believes its […]
item.content:encoded: On Thursday, TSMC’s Co-Chief Operating Officer Zhang Xiaoqiang stated at the North America Technology Symposium that the company has no plans to purchase ASML’s latest High-NA EUV lithography machines, citing their price of over €350 million per unit as too high. Although the equipment can improve chip manufacturing precision to below 1.4nm, TSMC believes its existing EUV systems are still sufficient to meet production needs. As ASML’s largest customer, TSMC’s decision could impact the company’s planned large-scale production rollout in 2027–2028, as well as its €60 billion revenue target for 2030. [TechWeb, in Chinese]

item.title: Auto China 2026: Beijing Auto Show opens as world’s largest, featuring 181 debut vehicles
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: The 2026 Beijing International Automotive Exhibition opened on Thursday, with organisers saying it is the world’s largest auto show by exhibition area. The event spans 380,000 square metres and features 1,451 vehicles, including 181 making their global debut and 71 concept cars. Held under the theme Driving the Future of Intelligence, the show highlights trends […]
item.content:encoded:
The 2026 Beijing International Automotive Exhibition opened on Thursday, with organisers saying it is the world’s largest auto show by exhibition area. The event spans 380,000 square metres and features 1,451 vehicles, including 181 making their global debut and 71 concept cars. Held under the theme Driving the Future of Intelligence, the show highlights trends such as intelligent driving, electrification and connectivity, with displays of advanced driver-assistance systems, in-car software and integrated smart mobility solutions.

More than 200 press conferences and a series of industry forums are scheduled during the exhibition, aimed at fostering global exchange and cooperation in the automotive sector. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: Tech Odyssey Series: From Parkinson’s research to flour analytics, how Porto turns lab work into startups
item.dc:creator: TechNode Staff
item.description: Previous on Tech Odyssey: In our last episode, we visited Omniflow and saw how the company turned a streetlight pole into a standardized piece of urban infrastructure with charging, connectivity, and sensing built in. This time, we head to the University of Porto to look at where companies like that can come from. At the […]
item.content:encoded:
Previous on Tech Odyssey: In our last episode, we visited Omniflow and saw how the company turned a streetlight pole into a standardized piece of urban infrastructure with charging, connectivity, and sensing built in. This time, we head to the University of Porto to look at where companies like that can come from.

At the University of Porto, one of the clearest startup stories does not begin with a founder pitch or a funding round. It begins in a lab working on neuroimaging, brain activity, Parkinson’s disease, epilepsy, and subtle movement changes linked to neurological disorders.

The company that emerged from that environment, Seedsight, is focused on something very different: grain analysis. Its platform is designed to assess wheat, maize, and other cereals before they enter factories, measuring quality, safety, and productivity while predicting flour extraction rates. The team said the company started with three people, has grown to eight, and plans to reach ten by the end of the year as it expands its digitized sample base and prepares for industrial deployment.

That leap says a lot about how innovation works in Porto. At the center of the process is INESC TEC, one of Portugal’s best-known research institutions. João Claro, who serves both as a professor at the University of Porto and as INESC TEC’s chairman and CEO, describes the institute in direct terms: scientific research and technological innovation are treated as equally important.

The point is not to force every project into a startup. It is to make sure research does not stop at publication if it can move into testing, application, licensing, partnerships, or, in some cases, spinouts. The institute says it launches one or two spinouts each year.

Seedsight is not the only example. INESC TEC’s work also shows up in robotics, where a mobile manipulator system has already been tested in a satellite manufacturing facility in France, and in mapping, where Voxelmaps combines lidar, camera data, and AI to build 3D voxel maps for autonomous driving, with enough precision to identify a tram catenary wire about one centimeter thick.

The same pattern extends into energy and agriculture, including work with China Three Gorges on power-system analysis and robotics for Portugal’s steep terrain and vineyard regions. The sectors are different, but the underlying logic is similar: technical work keeps moving until it finds a practical form.

That is where UPTEC fits in. Founded in 2007, the University of Porto’s science and technology park has supported more than 800 startups. Its role matters because it comes later in the sequence, after the research has already started to become a product, a platform, or a technical team with commercial direction.

Seen together, the University of Porto, INESC TEC, and UPTEC do not just describe an ecosystem in the abstract. They show the stages through which a company can become possible.

That is what makes Porto worth paying attention to. The most interesting part is not just that startups exist here. It is that the path behind them is unusually easy to see. A lab focused on Parkinson’s disease and epilepsy can also produce a company focused on flour analytics. In Porto, that does not read like a detour. It reads like a system doing exactly what it was built to do.

Next on Tech Odyssey: Next, we visit Portugal Ventures to see how a local investment institution in Portugal finds companies like these — and decides which ones are worth backing.
